3.1.1.body:
## SELECT WHERE Basic Queries

The `SELECT` statement is the foundation of SQL. Combined with `WHERE`, it lets you retrieve exactly the rows you need from a database.

```sql
-- Basic select with filtering
SELECT id, name, email, created_at
FROM users
WHERE status = 'active'
  AND created_at >= '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Using IN, BETWEEN, LIKE
SELECT product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE category IN ('electronics', 'books')
  AND price BETWEEN 10 AND 100
  AND product_name LIKE '%Pro%';

-- NULL handling
SELECT name FROM customers
WHERE phone IS NOT NULL;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- `WHERE` filters rows before they are returned; use comparison operators, `IN`, `BETWEEN`, `LIKE`, and `IS NULL`.
- `ORDER BY` sorts results; add `DESC` for descending. `LIMIT` caps the result set.
- Always be explicit about columns instead of using `SELECT *` in production code.
3.1.1.intuition: SELECT WHERE is like telling a librarian exactly which shelf to look on and which books to pull. You describe the data you want and the database fetches only matching rows.

exercises.title: Filter and Sort Users
exercises.description: Write a query to find all users whose email ends with '@gmail.com', created in 2024, sorted by name alphabetically.
exercises.hint: Use LIKE '%@gmail.com' combined with a date range in WHERE, then ORDER BY name ASC.

exercises.title: NULL-Safe Search
exercises.description: Write a query that returns products where the description is NULL or the price is under 5, showing product_name and price.
exercises.hint: Combine IS NULL and a comparison with OR; remember OR needs parentheses when mixed with AND.

problems.problem: Query returns no rows even though data exists.
problems.cause: Comparing against NULL with = instead of IS NULL, or mismatched string casing.
problems.solution: Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for null checks. Use ILIKE or LOWER() for case-insensitive matching in PostgreSQL.

problems.problem: Query is very slow on a large table.
problems.cause: No index on the columns used in the WHERE clause, or using functions on indexed columns.
problems.solution: Create indexes on frequently filtered columns. Avoid wrapping indexed columns in functions like LOWER() unless you have a functional index.

3.1.2.body:
## JOINs: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS

JOINs combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column. Choosing the right join type determines which rows appear in the result.

```sql
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows
SELECT o.id, c.name, o.total
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id;

-- LEFT JOIN: all left rows, NULLs for no match
SELECT c.name, o.id AS order_id
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both tables
SELECT a.name, b.name
FROM table_a a
FULL OUTER JOIN table_b b ON a.key = b.key;

-- CROSS JOIN: cartesian product
SELECT s.size, c.color
FROM sizes s CROSS JOIN colors c;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- INNER JOIN returns only matching rows. LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table.
- FULL OUTER JOIN keeps unmatched rows from both sides. CROSS JOIN produces every combination.
- Always specify the join condition with ON to avoid accidental cartesian products.
3.1.2.intuition: Think of JOINs as overlapping Venn diagrams: INNER is the overlap, LEFT keeps everything on the left plus the overlap, and FULL keeps both circles entirely.

exercises.title: Find Customers Without Orders
exercises.description: Write a query using LEFT JOIN to find all customers who have never placed an order.
exercises.hint: LEFT JOIN orders on customer_id, then filter WHERE orders.id IS NULL.

exercises.title: Product-Category Matrix
exercises.description: Use a CROSS JOIN to generate all possible combinations of product sizes and colors, then filter to only combinations that exist in inventory.
exercises.hint: Start with CROSS JOIN then add a WHERE EXISTS subquery checking inventory.

problems.problem: JOIN returns far more rows than expected.
problems.cause: The join key is not unique on one side, creating a many-to-many multiplication.
problems.solution: Verify cardinality of join keys. Use DISTINCT or aggregate if duplicates are expected, or add extra join conditions.

problems.problem: LEFT JOIN behaves like INNER JOIN.
problems.cause: A WHERE clause on the right table filters out NULLs, effectively converting it to an INNER JOIN.
problems.solution: Move the filter condition from WHERE into the ON clause of the LEFT JOIN.

3.1.3.body:
## Aggregations: GROUP BY and HAVING

Aggregation functions collapse multiple rows into summary values. GROUP BY defines the grouping and HAVING filters the aggregated results.

```sql
-- Count orders per customer
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count,
       SUM(total) AS total_spent,
       AVG(total) AS avg_order
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

-- Multiple grouping columns
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM created_at) AS yr,
       EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_at) AS mo,
       category, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM products
GROUP BY yr, mo, category;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Common aggregates: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX. COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(col) skips NULLs.
- Every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY.
- WHERE filters rows before aggregation; HAVING filters groups after aggregation.
3.1.3.intuition: GROUP BY is like sorting items into buckets, then measuring each bucket. HAVING is picking which buckets to keep based on those measurements.

exercises.title: Top Spending Categories
exercises.description: Write a query that shows each product category with its total revenue, but only categories with revenue over $10,000, sorted descending.
exercises.hint: GROUP BY category, SUM(price * quantity) AS revenue, HAVING revenue > 10000.

exercises.title: Monthly Active Users
exercises.description: Count distinct users who logged in each month in 2024. Return month and user_count.
exercises.hint: Use COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) and GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM login_date) with a WHERE on the year.

problems.problem: Error: column must appear in the GROUP BY clause or be used in an aggregate function.
problems.cause: A column in SELECT is neither grouped nor aggregated.
problems.solution: Add the column to GROUP BY or wrap it in an aggregate function like MAX() or ANY_VALUE().

problems.problem: HAVING clause seems to have no effect.
problems.cause: Confusing HAVING with WHERE; using HAVING on non-aggregated columns.
problems.solution: Use WHERE for row-level filtering before grouping, HAVING only for conditions on aggregate results.

3.1.4.body:
## Subqueries and Correlated Subqueries

Subqueries are queries nested inside another query. They can appear in SELECT, FROM, or WHERE clauses. Correlated subqueries reference columns from the outer query.

```sql
-- Scalar subquery in WHERE
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

-- Subquery in FROM (derived table)
SELECT dept, avg_sal
FROM (SELECT department AS dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
      FROM employees GROUP BY department) sub
WHERE avg_sal > 80000;

-- Correlated subquery with EXISTS
SELECT c.name FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM orders o
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.id
      AND o.total > 1000
);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Non-correlated subqueries run once; correlated subqueries run once per outer row.
- EXISTS is often faster than IN for large datasets because it short-circuits.
- Derived tables in FROM must have an alias.
3.1.4.intuition: A subquery is a question inside a question. A correlated subquery is like checking each student's grade against their own class average rather than the school average.

exercises.title: Above-Average Products
exercises.description: Find all products priced above the average price of their own category using a correlated subquery.
exercises.hint: In the WHERE clause, compare price to a subquery that computes AVG(price) for the same category.

exercises.title: Customers With Recent Orders
exercises.description: Use EXISTS to find customers who placed an order in the last 30 days.
exercises.hint: EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id AND order_date > CURRENT_DATE - 30).

problems.problem: Subquery returns more than one row when a scalar is expected.
problems.cause: Using = with a subquery that returns multiple rows instead of a single value.
problems.solution: Use IN, ANY, or ALL instead of =, or add LIMIT 1 / aggregation to ensure a single value.

problems.problem: Correlated subquery is extremely slow.
problems.cause: The subquery executes once per row in the outer query with no supporting index.
problems.solution: Add an index on the correlated column, or rewrite as a JOIN or CTE for better performance.

3.1.5.body:
## Window Functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD

Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them into a single output row.

```sql
-- ROW_NUMBER and RANK
SELECT name, department, salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
FROM employees;

-- LAG and LEAD for comparing adjacent rows
SELECT date, revenue,
  LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day,
  revenue - LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS daily_change,
  LEAD(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS next_day
FROM daily_sales;

-- Running total
SELECT date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_total
FROM transactions;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- PARTITION BY divides rows into groups; ORDER BY sets the sequence within each partition.
- ROW_NUMBER gives unique sequential numbers; RANK allows ties with gaps; DENSE_RANK has no gaps.
- LAG looks back, LEAD looks forward. Both take an optional offset and default value.
3.1.5.intuition: Window functions are like looking at a spreadsheet where each row can peek at other rows in its group to compute relative values, without merging the rows together.

exercises.title: Top 3 Per Department
exercises.description: Find the top 3 highest-paid employees in each department using ROW_NUMBER.
exercises.hint: Use a CTE with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC), then filter WHERE rn <= 3.

exercises.title: Month-over-Month Growth
exercises.description: Calculate the percentage change in monthly revenue compared to the previous month using LAG.
exercises.hint: Use (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) / LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) * 100.

problems.problem: Cannot use window function in WHERE clause.
problems.cause: SQL evaluates WHERE before window functions are computed.
problems.solution: Wrap the window function in a CTE or subquery, then filter in the outer query's WHERE clause.

problems.problem: ROW_NUMBER gives unexpected ordering with ties.
problems.cause: ROW_NUMBER assigns arbitrary order to tied values.
problems.solution: Add a tiebreaker column to ORDER BY, or use RANK/DENSE_RANK if ties should share the same number.

further_reading.title: Window Functions - Mode Analytics SQL Tutorial

3.1.6.body:
## Common Table Expressions (CTEs)

CTEs use the `WITH` clause to define temporary named result sets that make complex queries readable and composable.

```sql
-- Basic CTE
WITH active_users AS (
    SELECT id, name, email
    FROM users
    WHERE status = 'active'
)
SELECT au.name, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count
FROM active_users au
JOIN orders o ON au.id = o.user_id
GROUP BY au.name;

-- Chained CTEs
WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) AS month,
           SUM(amount) AS revenue
    FROM sales GROUP BY 1
),
growth AS (
    SELECT month, revenue,
           LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev
    FROM monthly
)
SELECT month, revenue,
       ROUND((revenue - prev) / prev * 100, 1) AS pct_growth
FROM growth;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- CTEs improve readability by breaking complex logic into named steps.
- Multiple CTEs can be chained with commas. Each can reference previously defined CTEs.
- Recursive CTEs use `WITH RECURSIVE` for tree or graph traversal.
3.1.6.intuition: CTEs are like naming intermediate variables in a math proof. Instead of one giant nested expression, you break it into labeled steps that build on each other.

exercises.title: Multi-Step Sales Report
exercises.description: Write chained CTEs: first compute daily totals, then weekly averages, then return weeks where the average exceeds $1000.
exercises.hint: CTE1 groups by date, CTE2 groups CTE1 by week with AVG, outer query filters HAVING avg > 1000.

exercises.title: Recursive Org Chart
exercises.description: Use WITH RECURSIVE to traverse an employees table (id, name, manager_id) and list all reports under a given manager.
exercises.hint: Base case: WHERE manager_id = :target. Recursive step: JOIN employees ON manager_id = cte.id.

problems.problem: Recursive CTE runs infinitely and times out.
problems.cause: Circular references in the data or missing termination condition.
problems.solution: Add a depth counter and WHERE depth < N to cap recursion, or use CYCLE detection in PostgreSQL 14+.

problems.problem: CTE performance is worse than a subquery.
problems.cause: In older PostgreSQL versions, CTEs act as optimization fences preventing predicate pushdown.
problems.solution: Use PostgreSQL 12+ which inlines non-recursive CTEs, or add MATERIALIZED/NOT MATERIALIZED hints.

3.2.1.body:
## PostgreSQL Data Types

PostgreSQL offers a rich set of data types beyond the standard SQL types. Choosing the right type ensures data integrity and query performance.

```sql
-- Numeric types
CREATE TABLE products (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    price NUMERIC(10, 2),
    weight DOUBLE PRECISION,
    quantity INTEGER DEFAULT 0
);

-- Text, date, and special types
CREATE TABLE events (
    id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    tags TEXT[],
    metadata JSONB,
    scheduled TIMESTAMPTZ,
    duration INTERVAL,
    location POINT
);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Use NUMERIC for money/exact math, INTEGER/BIGINT for counters, and DOUBLE PRECISION only when approximation is acceptable.
- TIMESTAMPTZ stores timezone-aware timestamps; always prefer it over TIMESTAMP.
- Arrays, JSONB, UUID, and range types give PostgreSQL flexibility beyond traditional RDBMS.
3.2.1.intuition: Data types are contracts with the database about what shape your data takes. Picking the right type means the database can validate, store, and index your data optimally.

exercises.title: Design a Users Table
exercises.description: Create a users table with appropriate types: UUID primary key, email, hashed password, login count, bio, and creation timestamp with timezone.
exercises.hint: Use UUID with gen_random_uuid(), VARCHAR for email, TEXT for bio, INTEGER for count, TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now().

exercises.title: Array Column Queries
exercises.description: Create a table with a TEXT[] tags column. Insert rows and query for rows where tags contain 'python' using the ANY operator.
exercises.hint: WHERE 'python' = ANY(tags) or use the @> operator with ARRAY['python'].

problems.problem: Floating point comparison returns unexpected results.
problems.cause: DOUBLE PRECISION stores approximate values; 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3.
problems.solution: Use NUMERIC for exact decimal arithmetic, especially for financial data.

problems.problem: TIMESTAMP shows wrong time in different timezones.
problems.cause: Using TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE loses timezone context.
problems.solution: Always use TIMESTAMPTZ. Set the session timezone with SET timezone = 'UTC' for consistency.

further_reading.title: Choosing the Right PostgreSQL Data Type

3.2.2.body:
## Indexes: B-tree, Hash, GIN, GiST, BRIN

Indexes speed up queries by creating optimized lookup structures. PostgreSQL supports multiple index types, each suited to different access patterns.

```sql
-- B-tree (default): equality and range queries
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email);

-- Hash: equality only, smaller than B-tree
CREATE INDEX idx_users_status ON users USING hash (status);

-- GIN: arrays, JSONB, full-text search
CREATE INDEX idx_products_tags ON products USING gin (tags);

-- GiST: geometric, range, proximity queries
CREATE INDEX idx_locations ON places USING gist (location);

-- BRIN: large naturally ordered tables
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_created ON logs USING brin (created_at);

-- Partial index
CREATE INDEX idx_active_users ON users (email)
  WHERE status = 'active';
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- B-tree handles most use cases: equality, range, sorting, and prefix LIKE queries.
- GIN excels for multi-valued columns (arrays, JSONB, text search). BRIN is compact for large sequential data.
- Partial and covering indexes reduce size and improve specificity.
3.2.2.intuition: An index is like a book's table of contents. Different index types are like different ways of organizing that TOC: alphabetical, by topic, by date, or by location.

exercises.title: Index Strategy Design
exercises.description: Given a query that filters by status='active' and orders by created_at, design an optimal composite index.
exercises.hint: CREATE INDEX ON table (status, created_at) or a partial index WHERE status = 'active' on created_at.

exercises.title: GIN Index for JSONB
exercises.description: Create a GIN index on a JSONB column and write a query that finds all rows where metadata contains a key 'role' with value 'admin'.
exercises.hint: CREATE INDEX USING gin (metadata); then WHERE metadata @> '{"role": "admin"}'.

problems.problem: Index exists but query does not use it.
problems.cause: Wrapping the indexed column in a function, casting types, or the planner estimating a sequential scan is faster.
problems.solution: Create a functional index matching the expression, or use SET enable_seqscan = off to test. Check with EXPLAIN.

problems.problem: Too many indexes slow down writes.
problems.cause: Each INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update every index on the table.
problems.solution: Audit unused indexes with pg_stat_user_indexes. Drop indexes with low idx_scan counts.

further_reading.title: The Missing Guide to PostgreSQL Indexes

3.2.3.body:
## EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Query Optimization

EXPLAIN ANALYZE executes a query and shows the actual execution plan, including row estimates, timing, and which indexes were used.

```sql
-- Basic EXPLAIN ANALYZE
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id)
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE u.status = 'active'
GROUP BY u.name;

-- Verbose output with buffers
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT * FROM products
WHERE category = 'electronics'
ORDER BY price;
```

Key plan nodes to understand:
- **Seq Scan**: full table scan, no index used
- **Index Scan / Index Only Scan**: using an index
- **Hash Join / Nested Loop / Merge Join**: join strategies
- **Sort**: explicit sorting step

**Key Takeaways:**
- Compare estimated vs actual rows; large mismatches indicate stale statistics (run ANALYZE).
- BUFFERS shows cache hit ratio; high shared hit means data is in memory.
- Look for the most expensive node (highest actual time) and optimize that first.
3.2.3.intuition: EXPLAIN ANALYZE is like an X-ray of your query. It shows exactly how the database decided to find your data and where it spent the most time.

exercises.title: Read a Query Plan
exercises.description: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a JOIN query with a WHERE clause. Identify whether an index scan or sequential scan is used and note the actual vs estimated rows.
exercises.hint: Look for 'Seq Scan' or 'Index Scan' nodes. Compare 'rows=' (estimated) with 'actual rows='.

exercises.title: Optimize a Slow Query
exercises.description: Given a query with a Seq Scan on a large table, add an appropriate index and verify the plan changes to Index Scan.
exercises.hint: Create an index on the filtered column, rerun EXPLAIN ANALYZE, and confirm the scan type changed.

problems.problem: Estimated rows are wildly different from actual rows.
problems.cause: Table statistics are outdated after bulk inserts or deletes.
problems.solution: Run ANALYZE on the table to refresh statistics. Adjust default_statistics_target for high-cardinality columns.

problems.problem: Query uses Nested Loop Join and is very slow.
problems.cause: Nested Loop is O(n*m) and poor for large tables without an index on the inner relation.
problems.solution: Ensure the inner table has an index on the join column. The planner may switch to Hash or Merge Join.

3.2.4.body:
## JSONB: Semi-Structured Data in PostgreSQL

JSONB stores binary-parsed JSON, enabling flexible schemas while retaining the power of SQL queries, indexing, and constraints.

```sql
CREATE TABLE events (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    data JSONB NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO events (data) VALUES
  ('{"type": "click", "page": "/home", "user_id": 42}');

-- Access nested fields
SELECT data->>'type' AS event_type,
       data->'metadata'->>'source' AS source
FROM events;

-- Containment query (uses GIN index)
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE data @> '{"type": "click"}';

-- Update nested value
UPDATE events
SET data = jsonb_set(data, '{metadata,processed}', 'true')
WHERE id = 1;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Use `->` to get JSON object, `->>` to get text. Use `@>` for containment checks.
- GIN indexes on JSONB columns make containment and existence queries fast.
- jsonb_set, jsonb_insert, and `||` operator allow partial updates without rewriting the whole value.
3.2.4.intuition: JSONB gives you the flexibility of a document database inside PostgreSQL. You get schemaless storage for parts of your data while keeping relational integrity everywhere else.

exercises.title: Query Nested JSONB
exercises.description: Given a JSONB column 'profile' with nested address fields, write a query to find all users in a specific city.
exercises.hint: Use profile->'address'->>'city' = 'Seattle' or containment: profile @> '{"address":{"city":"Seattle"}}'.

exercises.title: JSONB Aggregation
exercises.description: Build a JSONB object from query results using jsonb_agg and jsonb_build_object.
exercises.hint: SELECT jsonb_agg(jsonb_build_object('name', name, 'total', total)) FROM orders GROUP BY category.

problems.problem: JSONB query is slow despite having data indexed.
problems.cause: Using ->> operator with = instead of @> containment, which bypasses the GIN index.
problems.solution: Rewrite as a containment query with @>, or create an expression index on (data->>'field').

problems.problem: Lost data when updating JSONB with || operator.
problems.cause: The || merge is shallow and replaces entire nested objects rather than deep-merging.
problems.solution: Use jsonb_set() for targeted nested updates, or use || only for top-level keys.

further_reading.title: When to Use JSONB in PostgreSQL

3.2.5.body:
## Full-Text Search in PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL provides built-in full-text search with tsvector and tsquery, supporting stemming, ranking, and GIN-indexed fast lookups.

```sql
-- Create a tsvector column and index
ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector;
UPDATE articles SET search_vector =
  to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || body);
CREATE INDEX idx_fts ON articles USING gin (search_vector);

-- Search with tsquery
SELECT title, ts_rank(search_vector, q) AS rank
FROM articles, to_tsquery('english', 'python & web') q
WHERE search_vector @@ q
ORDER BY rank DESC;

-- Auto-update with trigger
CREATE TRIGGER trg_search_update
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON articles
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION
  tsvector_update_trigger(search_vector, 'pg_catalog.english', title, body);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- tsvector is a sorted list of normalized lexemes; tsquery defines the search terms with boolean operators.
- GIN index on tsvector makes full-text search performant on millions of rows.
- ts_rank and ts_rank_cd score relevance; use weights (A-D) to prioritize title over body.
3.2.5.intuition: Full-text search breaks text into normalized word stems and matches against search patterns. It is like having a mini search engine inside your database.

exercises.title: Weighted Search
exercises.description: Set up full-text search where title matches are weighted higher than body matches using setweight.
exercises.hint: setweight(to_tsvector(title), 'A') || setweight(to_tsvector(body), 'B') then use ts_rank.

exercises.title: Search with Phrase Matching
exercises.description: Write a query using phraseto_tsquery to find articles containing the exact phrase 'machine learning'.
exercises.hint: WHERE search_vector @@ phraseto_tsquery('english', 'machine learning').

problems.problem: Search returns no results for words that exist in the text.
problems.cause: Stemming reduces words to root forms; the exact word may not match the stemmed lexeme.
problems.solution: Check to_tsvector output to see the actual lexemes. Use plainto_tsquery for simpler matching.

problems.problem: Full-text search is slow on large tables.
problems.cause: Missing GIN index or computing tsvector on the fly in the query.
problems.solution: Store tsvector in a dedicated column, create a GIN index, and keep it updated with a trigger.

further_reading.title: Full-Text Search in PostgreSQL - Supabase Guide

3.3.1.body:
## Normalization: 1NF through 3NF

Normalization organizes tables to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity. Each normal form builds on the previous one.

```sql
-- UNNORMALIZED: repeating groups
-- orders(id, customer, item1, item2, item3) -- BAD

-- 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups
CREATE TABLE order_items (
    order_id INTEGER REFERENCES orders(id),
    product_id INTEGER REFERENCES products(id),
    quantity INTEGER,
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);

-- 2NF: remove partial dependencies
-- product_id -> product_name is partial dep, move to products

-- 3NF: remove transitive dependencies
-- employees(id, dept_id, dept_name) -- dept_name depends on dept_id
CREATE TABLE departments (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_id INTEGER REFERENCES departments(id)
);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- 1NF: each cell holds a single atomic value; rows are uniquely identifiable.
- 2NF: every non-key column depends on the entire primary key (eliminates partial dependencies).
- 3NF: non-key columns depend only on the key, not on other non-key columns.
3.3.1.intuition: Normalization means every fact is stored in exactly one place. If you change a department name, you update one row instead of thousands.

exercises.title: Identify Normal Form Violations
exercises.description: Given a table with columns (student_id, course_id, student_name, course_name, grade), identify which normal form it violates and fix it.
exercises.hint: student_name depends only on student_id (partial dep). Split into students, courses, and enrollments tables.

exercises.title: Normalize an Order System
exercises.description: Take a flat orders CSV with repeated customer info and product info. Design a normalized 3NF schema with proper foreign keys.
exercises.hint: Create customers, products, orders, and order_items tables. Each entity gets its own table.

problems.problem: Over-normalized schema requires too many JOINs and queries are slow.
problems.cause: Breaking every attribute into separate tables creates excessive join overhead.
problems.solution: Denormalize strategically for read-heavy workloads. Use materialized views to cache joined results.

problems.problem: Data anomalies: updating a customer name requires changing many rows.
problems.cause: Customer data is duplicated across the orders table (not normalized).
problems.solution: Extract customer data into a separate table and reference it with a foreign key.

3.3.2.body:
## ERD and Schema Design

Entity-Relationship Diagrams visually represent database tables, their columns, and relationships. Good schema design starts with understanding the domain.

```sql
CREATE TABLE customers (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES customers(id),
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE products (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL CHECK (price > 0)
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INTEGER REFERENCES orders(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    product_id INTEGER REFERENCES products(id),
    quantity INTEGER CHECK (quantity > 0)
);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Identify entities (nouns), attributes (properties), and relationships (verbs) from domain requirements.
- Use junction tables for many-to-many relationships.
- ON DELETE CASCADE, SET NULL, or RESTRICT controls referential integrity behavior.
3.3.2.intuition: An ERD is a blueprint for your database, just as an architect draws floor plans before building. It captures what exists and how things connect.

3.3.3.body:
## Constraints: PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK

Constraints enforce data integrity at the database level, preventing invalid data from ever being stored.

```sql
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    department_id INTEGER NOT NULL
        REFERENCES departments(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    salary NUMERIC CHECK (salary >= 0),
    start_date DATE NOT NULL,
    end_date DATE,
    CONSTRAINT valid_dates CHECK (end_date IS NULL OR end_date > start_date)
);

-- Composite unique constraint
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    student_id INTEGER REFERENCES students(id),
    course_id INTEGER REFERENCES courses(id),
    enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id)
);

-- Exclusion constraint (no overlapping date ranges)
CREATE TABLE bookings (
    room_id INTEGER,
    during TSTZRANGE,
    EXCLUDE USING gist (room_id WITH =, during WITH &&)
);
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- PRIMARY KEY = UNIQUE + NOT NULL. It identifies each row uniquely.
- FOREIGN KEY enforces referential integrity; the referenced row must exist.
- CHECK constraints validate column values. Exclusion constraints prevent overlapping ranges.
3.3.3.intuition: Constraints are guardrails for your data. They ensure the database rejects bad data rather than trusting every application to validate correctly.

problems.cause: Attempting to insert a row with a primary key or unique value that already exists.
problems.solution: Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (upsert) or ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.

problems.problem: Foreign key constraint blocks DELETE of referenced row.
problems.cause: Default ON DELETE behavior is RESTRICT.
problems.solution: Change to ON DELETE CASCADE or ON DELETE SET NULL, or delete dependent rows first.

3.3.4.body:
## Triggers, Views, and Stored Procedures

These database objects encapsulate logic at the database level, running automatically (triggers), simplifying queries (views), or providing reusable routines (procedures).

```sql
-- View: virtual table from a query
CREATE VIEW active_orders AS
SELECT o.id, c.name, o.total, o.created_at
FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.status = 'active';

-- Materialized view (cached)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_stats AS
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) AS month, SUM(total)
FROM orders GROUP BY 1;

-- Trigger: auto-update timestamp
CREATE FUNCTION update_modified() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
    NEW.updated_at = now();
    RETURN NEW;
END; $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER set_updated
BEFORE UPDATE ON orders
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_modified();

-- Stored procedure
CREATE PROCEDURE transfer_funds(from_id INT, to_id INT, amt NUMERIC)
LANGUAGE plpgsql AS $$
BEGIN
    UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - amt WHERE id = from_id;
    UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + amt WHERE id = to_id;
END; $$;
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Views simplify complex queries; materialized views cache results for performance.
- Triggers fire automatically on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE for auditing and timestamps.
- Stored procedures run multi-statement logic in a single CALL, reducing round trips.
3.3.4.intuition: Triggers, views, and procedures push logic into the database itself, so the rules are enforced no matter which application connects to it.

problems.solution: Use pg_trigger_depth() to prevent recursion, or restructure to avoid self-referencing updates.

problems.cause: Materialized views do not auto-refresh; they show data from the last REFRESH.

3.4.1.body:
## SQLAlchemy Core: Engine, Connection, Execution

The Engine is the starting point of SQLAlchemy. It manages the connection pool and provides the interface for executing SQL statements.

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

engine = create_engine(
    "postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost:5432/mydb",
    pool_size=5,
    max_overflow=10,
    echo=True
)

# Modern execution pattern (2.0 style)
with engine.connect() as conn:
    result = conn.execute(
        text("SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = :active"),
        {"active": True}
    )
    for row in result:
        print(row.id, row.name)
    conn.execute(
        text("UPDATE users SET last_login = now() WHERE id = :id"),
        {"id": 42}
    )
    conn.commit()

# Auto-commit block
with engine.begin() as conn:
    conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO logs (msg) VALUES (:m)"), {"m": "startup"})
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- create_engine does not open a connection immediately; connections are lazy and pooled.
- Always use `text()` with bound parameters (`:param`) to prevent SQL injection.
- `engine.begin()` provides a transaction context that auto-commits or rolls back.
3.4.1.intuition: The Engine is like a factory that hands out database connections from a pool. You borrow one, do your work, and return it -- the pool handles the lifecycle.

problems.cause: Connections are not being returned to the pool; missing context manager or explicit close.
problems.solution: Always use 'with engine.connect()' to ensure connections are returned. Increase pool_size if needed.

problems.cause: Using engine.connect() without calling conn.commit() after write operations.
problems.solution: Call conn.commit() explicitly or use engine.begin() which auto-commits on context exit.

3.4.2.body:
## SQLAlchemy Core: MetaData, Table, Column

MetaData is a collection of Table objects that describe your database schema in Python. Tables and columns are defined programmatically or reflected from the database.

```python
from sqlalchemy import MetaData, Table, Column, Integer, String, ForeignKey, DateTime, func

metadata = MetaData()

users = Table('users', metadata,
    Column('id', Integer, primary_key=True),
    Column('name', String(100), nullable=False),
    Column('email', String(255), unique=True, nullable=False),
    Column('created_at', DateTime, server_default=func.now())
)

posts = Table('posts', metadata,
    Column('id', Integer, primary_key=True),
    Column('user_id', Integer, ForeignKey('users.id'), nullable=False),
    Column('title', String(200)),
    Column('body', String)
)

metadata.create_all(engine)

# Reflect existing tables from database
metadata_reflected = MetaData()
metadata_reflected.reflect(bind=engine)
existing_table = metadata_reflected.tables['users']
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- MetaData holds all Table definitions; create_all() generates DDL only for tables that do not yet exist.
- Column types map to SQL types: Integer, String, Text, DateTime, Boolean, Numeric, etc.
- reflect() introspects an existing database and builds Table objects automatically.
3.4.2.intuition: MetaData is your schema catalog in Python. It knows every table and column, and can either define new ones or learn about existing ones by peeking at the database.

problems.problem: create_all() does not update existing tables with new columns.
problems.cause: create_all() only creates tables that do not exist; it does not alter existing ones.
problems.solution: Use Alembic migrations for schema changes, or manually run ALTER TABLE statements.

problems.problem: reflect() fails or returns empty tables.
problems.cause: Wrong schema name, or the database user lacks SELECT privileges on information_schema.
problems.solution: Specify schema='public' in reflect(), and ensure the DB user has adequate permissions.

3.4.3.body:
## SQLAlchemy SQL Expression Language

The expression language builds SQL statements in Python using Table and Column objects, giving you composable, database-agnostic queries.

```python
from sqlalchemy import select, insert, update, delete, func, and_, or_

stmt = (
    select(users.c.name, func.count(posts.c.id).label('post_count'))
    .join(posts, users.c.id == posts.c.user_id)
    .where(users.c.created_at >= '2024-01-01')
    .group_by(users.c.name)
    .having(func.count(posts.c.id) > 5)
    .order_by(func.count(posts.c.id).desc())
)

with engine.connect() as conn:
    rows = conn.execute(stmt).fetchall()

# INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
stmt = insert(users).values(name='Alice', email='alice@example.com')
stmt = update(users).where(users.c.id == 1).values(name='Bob')
stmt = delete(users).where(users.c.active == False)

# Composable: build queries dynamically
stmt = select(users)
if filter_active:
    stmt = stmt.where(users.c.active == True)
if search_name:
    stmt = stmt.where(users.c.name.ilike(f'%{search_name}%'))
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- select(), insert(), update(), delete() create statement objects that compile to SQL.
- Use `.c` to access columns: `table.c.column_name`. Chain .where(), .join(), .order_by().
- Statements are composable: add filters conditionally by reassigning the statement variable.
3.4.3.intuition: The expression language is like writing SQL with Python building blocks. Each method call adds a clause, and the final object compiles to proper SQL for your database.

3.4.4.body:
## Transactions and Connection Pooling

Transactions ensure atomicity: a group of operations either all succeed or all fail. Connection pooling reuses database connections for efficiency.

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

engine = create_engine(
    "postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/db",
    pool_size=10,
    max_overflow=20,
    pool_timeout=30,
    pool_recycle=1800
)

# Auto commit/rollback
with engine.begin() as conn:
    conn.execute(text("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1"))
    conn.execute(text("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2"))

# Manual transaction control
with engine.connect() as conn:
    trans = conn.begin()
    try:
        conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO orders (...) VALUES (...)"))
        conn.execute(text("UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE ..."))
        trans.commit()
    except Exception:
        trans.rollback()
        raise

# Nested savepoints
with engine.begin() as conn:
    conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO logs (msg) VALUES ('start')"))
    sp = conn.begin_nested()
    try:
        conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO risky_table VALUES (...)"))
        sp.commit()
    except Exception:
        sp.rollback()
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- engine.begin() provides a transaction that auto-commits on success and rolls back on exception.
- Connection pools avoid the overhead of creating new TCP connections for every request.
- Savepoints (begin_nested) allow partial rollback within a larger transaction.
3.4.4.intuition: Transactions are like an all-or-nothing promise. Connection pooling is like a carpool lane: sharing rides to the database so everyone gets there faster.

problems.solution: Always use context managers (with engine.connect()). Monitor pool with pool events and adjust pool_size.

problems.solution: Set pool_recycle to less than the database's wait_timeout. Enable pool_pre_ping=True for health checks.

3.5.1.body:
## Declarative Models and Mapped Columns

SQLAlchemy ORM maps Python classes to database tables using a declarative style. Each class attribute becomes a column.

```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship
from sqlalchemy import String, DateTime, func
from datetime import datetime

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'users'

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    email: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(255), unique=True)
    active: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(default=True)
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime, server_default=func.now()
    )
    bio: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(String)

    posts: Mapped[list['Post']] = relationship(back_populates='author')

    def __repr__(self):
        return f'<User(id={self.id}, name={self.name})>'

Base.metadata.create_all(engine)
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Mapped[type] with mapped_column() is the modern SQLAlchemy 2.0 pattern replacing Column().
- Type annotations control nullability: Mapped[str] is NOT NULL, Mapped[str | None] is nullable.
- __tablename__ sets the table name; Base.metadata.create_all() generates DDL.
3.5.1.intuition: Declarative models let you define database tables as Python classes. Each instance is a row, each class attribute is a column, and Python types map to SQL types.

3.5.2.body:
## Sessions: add, commit, rollback, flush

The Session is the ORM's workspace. It tracks changes to objects and synchronizes them with the database through flush and commit.

```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

with Session(engine) as session:
    user = User(name='Alice', email='alice@example.com')
    session.add(user)
    session.flush()           # sends INSERT, but no commit
    print(user.id)            # id is now available
    session.commit()          # persists the transaction

# Rollback on error
with Session(engine) as session:
    try:
        session.add(User(name='Bob', email='bob@example.com'))
        session.add(User(name='Dup', email='bob@example.com'))
        session.commit()
    except Exception:
        session.rollback()
        raise

# Bulk operations
with Session(engine) as session:
    session.add_all([
        User(name='C', email='c@x.com'),
        User(name='D', email='d@x.com')
    ])
    session.commit()

# Delete
with Session(engine) as session:
    user = session.get(User, 1)
    session.delete(user)
    session.commit()
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- add() stages an object; flush() sends SQL without committing; commit() finalizes the transaction.
- After rollback(), the session is usable again but previously pending objects are expunged.
- Use session.get(Model, pk) for primary key lookups; it checks the identity map first.
3.5.2.intuition: The Session is like a shopping cart. You add items (add), the store checks availability (flush), and you pay at checkout (commit). If something goes wrong, you empty the cart (rollback).

3.5.3.body:
## Relationships: One-to-Many, Many-to-Many

SQLAlchemy relationships define how models reference each other, enabling navigation between related objects without manual joins.

```python
from sqlalchemy import ForeignKey, Table, Column, Integer
from sqlalchemy.orm import Mapped, mapped_column, relationship

class Author(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'authors'
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    books: Mapped[list['Book']] = relationship(back_populates='author')

class Book(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'books'
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    title: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(200))
    author_id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(ForeignKey('authors.id'))
    author: Mapped['Author'] = relationship(back_populates='books')

# Many-to-Many
student_course = Table('student_course', Base.metadata,
    Column('student_id', Integer, ForeignKey('students.id'), primary_key=True),
    Column('course_id', Integer, ForeignKey('courses.id'), primary_key=True)
)

class Student(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'students'
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    courses: Mapped[list['Course']] = relationship(
        secondary=student_course, back_populates='students'
    )
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- back_populates links both sides so changes propagate bidirectionally.
- One-to-many uses a ForeignKey on the child. Many-to-many uses a secondary association table.
- cascade='all, delete-orphan' on the parent auto-deletes children when the parent is removed.
3.5.3.intuition: Relationships are bridges between models. Once defined, you can walk from an author to their books or a student to their courses without writing SQL.

3.5.4.body:
## Eager vs Lazy Loading

Loading strategies control when related objects are fetched from the database. The wrong strategy leads to N+1 queries or loading too much data.

```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import joinedload, selectinload, lazyload
from sqlalchemy import select

# N+1 problem with lazy loading (default)
with Session(engine) as session:
    authors = session.execute(select(Author)).scalars().all()
    for a in authors:
        print(a.books)  # each access fires a separate SELECT

# Fix: eager load with joinedload (single JOIN)
stmt = select(Author).options(joinedload(Author.books))
authors = session.execute(stmt).unique().scalars().all()

# selectinload: separate SELECT ... WHERE id IN (...)
stmt = select(Author).options(selectinload(Author.books))

# Nested eager loading
stmt = select(Author).options(
    selectinload(Author.books).joinedload(Book.publisher)
)

# Override at relationship level
class Author(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'authors'
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    books: Mapped[list['Book']] = relationship(
        back_populates='author', lazy='selectin'
    )
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Lazy loading (default) fires one query per access. Fine for single objects, terrible in loops.
- joinedload uses a SQL JOIN; best for single-child or mandatory relationships.
- selectinload fires a second SELECT with IN; best for collections to avoid cartesian products.
3.5.4.intuition: Lazy loading is like ordering one item at a time from a menu. Eager loading is like ordering everything up front. Choose based on whether you know you will need the related data.

problems.cause: A JOIN with a one-to-many relationship multiplies parent rows.
problems.solution: Call .unique() on the result before .scalars().all() to deduplicate, or use selectinload instead.

problems.cause: Eager loading large collections when only the parent data is needed.
problems.solution: Use raiseload() to make accidental lazy loads raise an error, forcing explicit loading decisions.

3.5.5.intuition: Hybrid properties give your model methods that speak two languages: Python for instances and SQL for queries. You define the logic once and use it everywhere.

3.6.1.body:
## Alembic Setup and Configuration

Alembic is the migration tool for SQLAlchemy. It tracks schema changes as versioned migration scripts, enabling reproducible database evolution.

```python
# Initialize Alembic in your project
# $ pip install alembic
# $ alembic init alembic

# alembic.ini - set the database URL
# sqlalchemy.url = postgresql://user:pass@localhost/mydb

# alembic/env.py - configure target metadata
from myapp.models import Base
target_metadata = Base.metadata

def run_migrations_online():
    connectable = engine_from_config(
        config.get_section(config.config_ini_section),
        prefix="sqlalchemy."
    )
    with connectable.connect() as connection:
        context.configure(
            connection=connection,
            target_metadata=target_metadata
        )
        with context.begin_transaction():
            context.run_migrations()

# Using environment variable for DB URL
import os
config.set_main_option('sqlalchemy.url', os.environ['DATABASE_URL'])
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- `alembic init` creates the alembic/ directory with env.py and a versions/ folder.
- Set target_metadata in env.py to your Base.metadata so Alembic can compare models to the database.
- Store the database URL in environment variables, not in alembic.ini, for security.
3.6.1.intuition: Alembic is version control for your database schema. Just as git tracks code changes, Alembic tracks CREATE TABLE, ALTER COLUMN, and other DDL as sequential scripts.

exercises.hint: Run alembic init alembic, edit env.py to import your Base, set target_metadata = Base.metadata.

problems.solution: Ensure env.py imports the correct Base. Add your project to sys.path if needed.

problems.cause: sqlalchemy.url in alembic.ini still has the default placeholder.
problems.solution: Set the URL in alembic.ini or override it in env.py using config.set_main_option().

3.6.2.body:
## Creating and Running Migrations

Migrations are scripts that evolve your database schema. Each migration has an upgrade (apply) and downgrade (revert) function.

```python
# Generate a migration
# $ alembic revision -m "add users table"

from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

def upgrade():
    op.create_table(
        'users',
        sa.Column('id', sa.Integer, primary_key=True),
        sa.Column('name', sa.String(100), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('email', sa.String(255), unique=True),
        sa.Column('created_at', sa.DateTime, server_default=sa.func.now())
    )
    op.create_index('idx_users_email', 'users', ['email'])

def downgrade():
    op.drop_index('idx_users_email')
    op.drop_table('users')

# Run migrations
# $ alembic upgrade head       # apply all pending
# $ alembic upgrade +1         # apply next one
# $ alembic downgrade -1       # revert last one
# $ alembic current            # show current revision
# $ alembic history            # show all revisions
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Each migration gets a unique revision ID. Alembic tracks the current version in an alembic_version table.
- Always write both upgrade() and downgrade() for reversibility.
- Use op.add_column, op.drop_column, op.alter_column, op.create_index for schema changes.
3.6.2.intuition: Migrations are like numbered instructions for building your database. You can fast-forward to any point or rewind step by step.

exercises.hint: Add column first, then use op.execute("UPDATE users SET full_name = first_name || ' ' || last_name").

problems.cause: Some operations like CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction.
problems.solution: Test migrations on a staging database first. For non-transactional DDL, use op.execute() outside the transaction.

problems.problem: Downgrade does not fully revert the upgrade.
problems.cause: Downgrade function is incomplete or does not reverse data changes.
problems.solution: Always test downgrade after upgrade. Include data rollback logic, not just schema rollback.

3.6.3.body:
## Autogenerate and Branching Strategies

Alembic can auto-detect differences between your models and the database, and it supports branching and merging for team workflows.

```python
# Autogenerate migration from model changes
# $ alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add posts table"

# Autogenerate detects:
# - New/dropped tables, added/removed columns
# - Changed column types, nullable, server_default
# - Added/removed indexes and foreign keys

# Autogenerate does NOT detect:
# - Table or column renames (seen as drop + add)
# - Changes to CHECK constraints or triggers

# Branch management
# $ alembic heads              # show all branch heads
# $ alembic merge -m "merge"   # merge two branches

# versions/abc_branch1.py
revision = 'abc'
down_revision = 'base_rev'

# versions/def_branch2.py
revision = 'def'
down_revision = 'base_rev'  # same parent = branch

# Merge migration
# $ alembic merge abc def -m "merge branches"
# Creates: down_revision = ('abc', 'def')
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- `--autogenerate` compares model metadata to the live database and generates migration ops.
- Always review autogenerated migrations; they may miss renames or produce incorrect diffs.
- Use `alembic merge` when two developers create migrations from the same base revision.
3.6.3.intuition: Autogenerate is like a diff tool for your database schema. Branching lets multiple developers evolve the schema in parallel, merging when ready.

exercises.hint: alembic revision --autogenerate -m 'description', then inspect the generated upgrade/downgrade functions.

exercises.hint: Create two revisions with the same down_revision, run alembic heads, then alembic merge.

problems.problem: Autogenerate creates drop + create instead of rename.
problems.cause: Alembic cannot detect renames; it sees the old table gone and a new one added.
problems.solution: Manually edit the migration to use op.rename_table() instead of drop + create.

problems.problem: Multiple heads error when running alembic upgrade head.
problems.cause: Two migration branches exist without a merge migration.
problems.solution: Run alembic merge <rev1> <rev2> -m 'merge' to create a merge point, then upgrade.

3.7.1.body:
## Redis Data Structures

Redis is an in-memory data store supporting rich data structures: strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and more.

```python
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Strings
r.set('user:1:name', 'Alice', ex=3600)
r.incr('page:views')

# Hashes
r.hset('user:1', mapping={'name': 'Alice', 'email': 'a@b.com', 'age': '30'})
user = r.hgetall('user:1')

# Lists
r.rpush('queue:tasks', 'task1', 'task2', 'task3')
task = r.lpop('queue:tasks')

# Sets
r.sadd('tags:post:1', 'python', 'redis', 'backend')
common = r.sinter('tags:post:1', 'tags:post:2')

# Sorted Sets
r.zadd('leaderboard', {'alice': 100, 'bob': 85, 'carol': 92})
top3 = r.zrevrange('leaderboard', 0, 2, withscores=True)
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Each data structure has O(1) or O(log N) operations optimized for its use case.
- Hashes are ideal for object storage. Lists work as queues. Sorted sets enable leaderboards.
- All operations are atomic. Use `ex` or `px` parameters for TTL-based expiration.
3.7.1.intuition: Redis data structures are like specialized containers in memory. Pick the right container for your pattern: a dict for objects, a list for queues, a sorted set for rankings.

exercises.hint: zadd for scores, zrevrange(0, 9) for top 10, zrevrank for position, zincrby to update.

problems.problem: Redis returns bytes instead of strings in Python.
problems.cause: Default redis-py client returns bytes.
problems.solution: Pass decode_responses=True when creating the Redis client.

problems.problem: Memory usage grows unbounded.
problems.cause: Keys are created without TTL and never cleaned up.
problems.solution: Set TTL on all ephemeral keys. Use maxmemory and eviction policies like allkeys-lru.

3.7.2.body:
## Caching Patterns: Cache-Aside, Write-Through, TTL

Caching reduces database load by storing frequently accessed data in Redis. The caching pattern determines when and how the cache is populated.

```python
import redis, json

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
def get_user(user_id: int) -> dict:
    cache_key = f'user:{user_id}'
    cached = r.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)
    user = db.query(User).get(user_id)
    user_data = {'id': user.id, 'name': user.name}
    r.set(cache_key, json.dumps(user_data), ex=300)
    return user_data

# Write-Through
def update_user(user_id: int, data: dict):
    db.query(User).filter_by(id=user_id).update(data)
    db.commit()
    r.set(f'user:{user_id}', json.dumps(data), ex=300)

# Cache Invalidation
def delete_user(user_id: int):
    db.query(User).filter_by(id=user_id).delete()
    db.commit()
    r.delete(f'user:{user_id}')
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Cache-aside loads data into cache only on a miss; simple but can serve stale data.
- Write-through updates cache on every write; consistent but adds write latency.
- TTL is your safety net: even if invalidation fails, stale data expires automatically.
3.7.2.intuition: Caching is a trade-off between speed and freshness. Cache-aside is reactive, write-through is proactive. TTL ensures nothing stays stale forever.

exercises.description: Write a cache-aside decorator that checks Redis before calling the database function, caching the result with a configurable TTL.
exercises.hint: Use functools.wraps, generate a cache key from function name + args, json serialize the result.

exercises.title: Cache Stampede Prevention
exercises.description: Implement a locking mechanism to prevent multiple processes from rebuilding the same cache key simultaneously.
exercises.hint: Use r.set(lock_key, 1, nx=True, ex=10) as a distributed lock. Only the lock holder rebuilds the cache.

problems.problem: Stale data persists in cache after a database update.
problems.cause: The cache was not invalidated or updated when the database was written to.
problems.solution: Invalidate or update the cache key on every write. Use TTL as a fallback safety net.

problems.problem: Cache stampede: many requests rebuild the same key simultaneously.
problems.cause: A popular cache key expires and all concurrent requests hit the database at once.
problems.solution: Use a distributed lock or probabilistic early expiration to stagger cache rebuilds.

3.7.3.body:
## Pub/Sub and Lua Scripting in Redis

Pub/Sub enables real-time messaging between services. Lua scripting executes atomic multi-step operations server-side.

```python
import redis, threading

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Publisher
r.publish('notifications', 'New order received')

# Subscriber (runs in its own thread)
def subscribe_loop():
    pubsub = r.pubsub()
    pubsub.subscribe('notifications', 'alerts')
    for message in pubsub.listen():
        if message['type'] == 'message':
            print(f"[{message['channel']}] {message['data']}")

thread = threading.Thread(target=subscribe_loop, daemon=True)
thread.start()

# Lua scripting: atomic rate limiter
rate_limit_script = """
local key = KEYS[1]
local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local window = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local current = tonumber(redis.call('GET', key) or '0')
if current >= limit then
    return 0
end
redis.call('INCR', key)
if current == 0 then
    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, window)
end
return 1
"""
rate_limit = r.register_script(rate_limit_script)
allowed = rate_limit(keys=['rate:user:42'], args=[10, 60])
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget; messages are lost if no subscriber is listening. Use Streams for persistence.
- Lua scripts execute atomically on the server, avoiding race conditions in multi-step operations.
- register_script() caches the script SHA for efficient repeated execution.
3.7.3.intuition: Pub/Sub is like a radio broadcast: anyone tuned in hears the message. Lua scripting is like handing the server a recipe card to execute all steps without interruption.

exercises.title: Chat Room with Pub/Sub
exercises.description: Build a simple chat system where users subscribe to a room channel and publish messages to it.
exercises.hint: Each user runs pubsub.subscribe('room:general'). Publishing is r.publish('room:general', message).

exercises.title: Atomic Token Bucket
exercises.description: Implement a token bucket rate limiter using a Lua script that atomically checks and decrements available tokens.
exercises.hint: Lua script: GET tokens, if > 0 DECR and return 1, else return 0. Use EXPIRE for refill window.

problems.problem: Subscriber misses messages published before it connected.
problems.cause: Pub/Sub does not buffer messages; only active subscribers receive them.
problems.solution: Use Redis Streams (XADD/XREAD) for durable message queues with consumer groups.

problems.problem: Lua script blocks all other Redis commands while running.
problems.cause: Redis is single-threaded; Lua scripts hold the event loop.
problems.solution: Keep Lua scripts short and fast. Avoid unbounded loops or large data operations in scripts.

3.7.4.body:
## Redis in Python: redis-py

redis-py is the standard Python client for Redis, supporting sync/async operations, connection pooling, pipelines, and all Redis data types.

```python
import redis

pool = redis.ConnectionPool(
    host='localhost', port=6379, db=0,
    max_connections=20, decode_responses=True
)
r = redis.Redis(connection_pool=pool)

# Pipeline: batch commands for fewer round trips
with r.pipeline() as pipe:
    pipe.set('key1', 'val1')
    pipe.set('key2', 'val2')
    pipe.get('key1')
    results = pipe.execute()  # [True, True, 'val1']

# Transaction with optimistic locking
with r.pipeline(transaction=True) as pipe:
    pipe.watch('balance:1')
    balance = int(r.get('balance:1'))
    pipe.multi()
    pipe.set('balance:1', balance - 100)
    pipe.set('balance:2', balance + 100)
    pipe.execute()

# Async support
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

async def async_example():
    r = aioredis.from_url('redis://localhost')
    await r.set('async_key', 'value')
    val = await r.get('async_key')
    await r.aclose()
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Use ConnectionPool for multi-threaded apps; redis-py is thread-safe with pooling.
- Pipelines batch multiple commands into a single round trip, reducing latency.
- Use pipeline(transaction=True) with WATCH for optimistic concurrency control.
3.7.4.intuition: redis-py wraps the Redis protocol in Pythonic APIs. Pipelines are like batching HTTP requests, and WATCH/MULTI gives you optimistic transactions.

exercises.title: Pipeline Performance
exercises.description: Benchmark inserting 10,000 keys with individual SET calls vs. using a pipeline. Compare execution times.
exercises.hint: Use time.time() around both approaches. Pipeline should be 5-10x faster due to fewer round trips.

exercises.title: Async Redis with FastAPI
exercises.description: Create a FastAPI endpoint that reads/writes to Redis using redis.asyncio for non-blocking operations.
exercises.hint: Create aioredis client at startup, inject via dependency, await r.get/r.set in the endpoint.

problems.problem: WatchError during transaction.
problems.cause: Another client modified the watched key between WATCH and EXEC.
problems.solution: Retry the transaction in a loop. This is expected with optimistic locking; keep the WATCH-EXEC window small.

problems.problem: Connection refused or timeout errors in production.
problems.cause: Redis server is down, network issues, or connection pool exhausted.
problems.solution: Use retry_on_timeout=True, health_check_interval, and monitor pool usage. Add circuit breaker logic.

3.8.1.body:
## MongoDB: Document Model and CRUD

MongoDB stores data as flexible JSON-like documents (BSON) in collections. CRUD operations work on individual documents or in bulk.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient

client = MongoClient('mongodb://localhost:27017')
db = client['myapp']
collection = db['users']

# Create
result = collection.insert_one({
    'name': 'Alice', 'email': 'alice@example.com',
    'age': 30, 'tags': ['admin', 'active']
})
collection.insert_many([{'name': 'Bob'}, {'name': 'Carol'}])

# Read
user = collection.find_one({'email': 'alice@example.com'})
active = collection.find({'tags': 'active'}).sort('name', 1).limit(10)

# Update
collection.update_one(
    {'_id': result.inserted_id},
    {'$set': {'age': 31}, '$push': {'tags': 'verified'}}
)

# Delete
collection.delete_one({'name': 'Bob'})
collection.delete_many({'tags': 'inactive'})
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Documents are schema-flexible; fields can vary between documents in the same collection.
- Use `$set`, `$push`, `$inc`, `$unset` operators for partial updates without rewriting the whole document.
- find() returns a cursor; chain .sort(), .limit(), .skip() for pagination.
3.8.1.intuition: MongoDB collections are like filing cabinets where each folder can have different contents. You query by describing what the document looks like rather than joining tables.

problems.cause: find() lazily returns a cursor for memory efficiency.
problems.solution: Use list(collection.find(...)) to materialize results, or iterate the cursor directly.

problems.problem: Update replaces the entire document.
problems.cause: Passing a plain dict to update_one without $set replaces the document (except _id).
problems.solution: Always use update operators like $set, $push, $inc for partial updates.

3.8.2.intuition: The aggregation pipeline is an assembly line for data. Each stage takes documents in, transforms them, and passes them to the next stage.

problems.cause: No index on fields used in $match, or $match is not the first stage.
problems.solution: Place $match as early as possible so it can use indexes. Create indexes on filtered fields.

3.8.3.body:
## MongoDB: Indexes, Schema Design, and pymongo

Proper indexing and schema design are critical for MongoDB performance. pymongo provides the full driver API for Python applications.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient, ASCENDING, DESCENDING

db = MongoClient()['myapp']

# Create indexes
db.users.create_index('email', unique=True)
db.users.create_index([('status', ASCENDING), ('created_at', DESCENDING)])

# Text index for search
db.articles.create_index([('title', 'text'), ('body', 'text')])
results = db.articles.find({'$text': {'$search': 'python mongodb'}})

# TTL index for auto-expiry
db.sessions.create_index('expires_at', expireAfterSeconds=0)

# Schema design: embed vs reference
# Embed: data accessed together, 1:few
{'name': 'Alice', 'addresses': [
    {'street': '123 Main', 'city': 'Seattle'}
]}
# Reference: many:many, large sub-documents
{'title': 'My Post', 'author_id': ObjectId('...')}

# Bulk operations
from pymongo import InsertOne, UpdateOne
ops = [InsertOne({'x': 1}), UpdateOne({'x': 1}, {'$inc': {'x': 1}})]
db.col.bulk_write(ops, ordered=False)
```

**Key Takeaways:**
- Create indexes on fields used in queries, sorts, and unique constraints.
- Embed related data when it is always accessed together. Reference when data is shared or large.
- TTL indexes auto-delete expired documents, perfect for sessions and temporary data.
3.8.3.intuition: MongoDB schema design is about co-locating data that is read together. Embed for speed, reference for flexibility. Indexes make or break query performance just like in SQL.

exercises.title: Design an E-Commerce Schema
exercises.description: Design MongoDB collections for products, orders, and users. Decide which data to embed vs reference.
exercises.hint: Embed order_items in orders (always read together). Reference user_id in orders (user data changes independently).

exercises.title: Index Optimization
exercises.description: Given slow queries on a collection, use explain() to identify missing indexes and create appropriate compound indexes.
exercises.hint: collection.find({...}).explain() shows queryPlanner. Look for COLLSCAN (no index) vs IXSCAN (index used).

problems.problem: Document exceeds 16MB BSON size limit.
problems.cause: Embedding too many items in an array within a single document.
problems.solution: Use the bucket pattern or reference pattern. Store large arrays in a separate collection.

problems.problem: Query does not use the expected compound index.
problems.cause: Query fields do not match the index prefix. A compound index (a, b, c) only helps queries starting with a.
problems.solution: Order compound index fields by equality filters first, then sort, then range filters (ESR rule).
